planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil warms above 60°F; a prolific variety that sets fruit well in warm, humid conditions, making it excellent for Gulf Coast and zone 8–10 gardens.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost and transplant to the garden once nighttime temperatures stay reliably above 55°F; Hungarian Hot Wax is a medium-heat open-pollinated pepper (5,000–10,000 Scoville units) that produces waxy, pale-yellow 5–7 inch fruits that ripen through orange to deep red, and it is among the most versatile peppers for pickling, stuffing, frying, and fresh eating. Fruits are ready to pick at the yellow stage for the mildest heat and thickest walls, or left to ripen red for maximum color and slightly more intensity. This variety is highly productive and disease-tolerant, thriving in both humid and dryer conditions; plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering once plants are established.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk passes and soil has warmed; this Bulgarian heirloom produces carrot-shaped, brilliant orange fruits that are thin-walled but very prolific.

directions: Start seed at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost in a moist starting mix and pot up once the first true leaves appear; transplant after frost danger passes and begin fertilizing with a low-nitrogen, high-phosphorus fertilizer to encourage fruit set. Bulgarian Carrot is an eastern European heirloom producing vivid orange, carrot-shaped fruits 3–4 inches long with thin, crispy walls and a surprising fruity, almost apricot-like sweetness that precedes a sharp, clean, medium heat (5,000 SHU). It is exceptionally productive, with dozens of fruits per plant, and is highly ornamental as fruits turn from yellow to brilliant orange at maturity. Use fresh in salsas and relishes where the fruity flavor is showcased, or pickle whole for a pantry staple.

common_name: Corno di Toro Sweet Italian Pepper

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into rich, well-drained soil after soil warms; this Italian heirloom is named 'bull's horn' for its long, curved sweet fruits and is among the finest frying peppers in the world.

directions: Sow seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost, harden off transplants over a week, and plant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after frost risk has passed. Corno di Toro — literally 'bull's horn' in Italian — is a prized sweet frying pepper producing curved, tapered fruits 7–10 inches long that ripen from green through yellow or red depending on the type, with thick, juicy, sweet walls and virtually no heat. Sauté in olive oil until lightly charred and serve with salt for the classic Italian preparation, or roast and peel for a sweet pepper preserve. The sweet-green stage gives the most crisp texture; the fully ripened red or yellow fruit is sweeter and milder. Extremely productive — a single plant can yield 30–50 fruits per season.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed; cherry peppers are very heat-tolerant and productive in warm climates, making them well-suited to zone 8–11 summer conditions.

directions: Start seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost in warm starting mix; transplant to fertile, well-drained soil after all frost risk has passed and soil temperature reaches 60°F. Sweet cherry peppers produce round, apple-sized fruits 1.5–2 inches in diameter that ripen from green to deep red and have thick, sweet, crisp walls with mild to no heat, making them among the best peppers for stuffing, pickling, and eating fresh off the plant. The compact, bushy plants are ornamental and prolific, producing dozens of round fruits per plant over a long season. Stuff raw fruits with cream cheese or pickled vegetables for appetizers, or pack them whole into brine for a classic Italian condiment.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; an early-maturing pimento-type pepper that performs well in shorter seasons; transplant after all frost has passed and soil warms to 60°F.

directions: Start seeds at 75–85°F 8–10 weeks before last frost and transplant into warm, rich, well-drained soil after all frost risk has passed. Lipstick is an early-maturing sweet pimento-type pepper that produces heart-shaped, 4-inch fruits with thick, candy-sweet walls, minimal seed cavity, and zero heat; they ripen from green to a brilliant lipstick red earlier than most pimentos, making it one of the better sweet pepper choices for northern gardens and short-season growers. The fruits are remarkably thick-walled and sweet when roasted, and they peel cleanly after charring, making them ideal for preserving as roasted red peppers. Compact plants are well-suited to containers.
